data.title: AGQ: More Risk Than Reward Going Into Summer (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My last ProShares UltraPro Silver ETF (AGQ) article was a Buy suggestion in August 2023 here. Of course, back then nobody was interested in writing/reading about an approaching silver (XAGUSD:CUR) rally. However, this ETF's 2X leverage to a monster silver upmove since early 2024 has translated into spectacular returns for trust holders. AGQ's tremendous +311% price gain since that article handily crushed the equivalent period S&P 500 increase of +63%.

In fact, several years ago I was calling for silver prices as high as US$100 oz., when almost nobody believed $30 was possible, both authors and commenters on Seeking Alpha. (Silver reached an all-time peak of $120+ in January 2026.)

The bad news is the bull run is taking a breather now, and such could last another 3-6 months in my estimation. Too much excitement, too much hot money, too much of a good thing, I suppose.

Commodity markets rarely move in a straight line, and when they do, it's often intelligent to walk away. I have been suggesting such since late December here, and other gold/silver miner articles since the blow-off topping formation. I even sold off a large chunk of my physical silver (coins) around $86 an ounce in January.

2X Leverage Kills In A Bear Market

The problem with AGQ and any 2X leveraged-long ETF is bear markets in the underlying index or commodity prove quite fatal for investors. Typically, with extra costs to create the leverage, including decaying futures and forward contracts, ETF pricing can decline more than its leveraged-design equivalent to a 200% long exposure position. That's what has happened with AGQ since the January 28th silver peak.

The bad news for AGQ holders is time is not working on your side, like a traditional blue-chip equity investment on Wall Street. Not only do you have to contend with the immediate downside price risk of silver falling instead of rising, but the long-term costs to generate 2X leverage dramatically reduce returns.

Below I have drawn the rotten AGQ performance since January 28th's silver top. I am comparing total returns (including occasional dividends on cash holdings) to the 1X leverage found in the iShares Silver Trust (SLV). AGQ's -68.1% total return over roughly three months has been more than double SLV's -32.7% drop.

Next, I have drawn 5-year and 10-year charts comparing investment performance between the two. Notice the 1X design has bested AGQ over each span, all caused by extra operating expenses through time (alongside some daily compounding math issues). This makes silver market timing even more critical when putting AGQ in your portfolio.

Lastly, since 2009 AGQ has been an abysmal performer vs. SLV. Over 17 years, AGQ has only been able to climb by +159.6% in total value vs. SLV's +644.9% advance! To boot, the current AGQ $119 price stands far away from the split-adjusted all-time number above $720 in April 2011.

I highly suggest potential investors in AGQ read all of the disclaimers on the ETFs Fact Sheet here. Another important tax consideration is waiting for the K-1 form to be mailed/sent to you before the April 15th tax filing deadline. I have picked out some of the important ProShares information for review below.

Final Thoughts

I am projecting another large downdraw in silver is approaching, which could potentially lead to a longer-term bottom for bulls. A break below silver's 200-day moving average to a price under $60, perhaps as low as $50 seems to be the next major swing. I am hoping my 40 years of experience watching and trading precious metals through all kinds of macroeconomic and political circumstances guides me to the correct (although not mainstream) view of what's about to play out.

If this forecast is the future, I would expect AGQ to trade well under its 200-day moving average as well. My target price zone into late summer is $65 to $80, boxed in green below. In other words, if you were too emotional in your buy decision process or just new and unlucky to silver asset trading, buying AGQ at $425 in late January and riding it all the way to potentially $80 or less over less than six months, could be your unfortunate result for chasing a boom late in the rally. It should provide a life lesson on owning 2X leverage during a wicked commodity dump. I have been trying to persuade readers to avoid this situation for many months running.

How could my short-term bearishness on AGQ be proven the wrong investment choice? Fair question. If a new geopolitical reality supplants the Iran war news, silver could experience a heightened level of flight-to-safety money flows. Say, China invading Taiwan, or Russia attacking a NATO country may be required (in my opinion) to reverse the bearish silver setup.

More than likely, the scenario to drive silver lower will be a continuation of the crude oil spike causing worries about central bank tightening on unexpected inflation increases. The Iran war could easily encourage tighter conditions in the credit/bond markets globally regardless of U.S. Federal Reserve actions, maybe even a bear market in equities and recession into the summertime.

Silver pricing trends tend to follow: (1) industrial demand and (2) investor flows hedging worries about paper currency values. Coming off the most overbought condition for silver since early 1980, weaker industrial demand in a global recession on top of skittish investors/speculators selling silver to raise funds would be a double whammy difficult for silver's price to escape. The rising exchange margin requirements for futures trading since December have also been a major negative for price, like I have been explaining since the end of 2025.

Consequently, I feel an official downgraded rating for ProShares Ultra Silver to Sell from Buy seems to be in order. I would sell and wait patiently for lower pricing in AGQ before considering it a smart place to park money again.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: STMicroelectronics: Strong Q1 Confirms Cycle Turn And Upside Potential
data.description:
Our readers know that we have long covered STMicroelectronics (STM). In hindsight, it may seem straightforward to highlight, but this was a particularly strong call, and the pace of the share price appreciation (almost +80%) over such a short period (January 2026) exceeded our initial expectations (Fig. 1). As a reminder, the company did not provide an outlook for 2025, and we reiterated a "Neutral Stance Amid Earnings Pressure And Limited Visibility" in commenting on STM H1 2025 results.

Our recent buy rating was supported by a gradual improvement in underlying fundamentals and the prolonged share price weakness. The company has a strong balance sheet, and with underappreciated AI opportunities and moderated automotive pressures, there was a basis to regain operating leverage (and move our rating to Buy).

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Stance

STM top-line sales reached $3.10 billion, with a year-on-year increase of 23% (Fig. 2). The company reported supportive results in the Microcontrollers segment, thanks to a rebound in Personal Electronics. Similarly, the Analog, MEMS, and Sensors segment grew a powerful +23.2%. The laggard remains the Power and Discrete (P&D) segment, with a 1.8% decline. That said, gross profit reached $1.05 billion, up 24.3% from last year. When gross profit margin increases at a higher rate than sales, it means the company is gaining back operating leverage. In the headline, STM reported negative FCF evolution; however, free cash flow included an $895 million cash outflow from the acquisition of NXP’s sensor segment.

Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, STM reported its strongest earnings beat in almost three years. This reinforces our positive view that the cycle is turning. In our view, this marks the early phase of a multi-quarter upgrade cycle. This will be supported by cyclical recovery and additional upside from customer-driven demand, notably in AI data centers and low Earth orbit satellites (we are recently deep-diving into the sector with our cross-coverage in SES S.A.).

We also reported the positive STM's press release. They communicated that “in Q1, despite the macroeconomic uncertainty, we saw improving demand with strong booking and normalized inventory in distribution.” For Q2, the company is guiding top-line sales growth of around 12% quarter over quarter. Looking at the history, this is above seasonality and indicates a recovery story in place. STM top management is setting expectations for H2, anticipating a more normal seasonal pattern while still implying 19% year-on-year growth. Again, even if this comment is read as undermining, STM has faced allegations that it has not reported the correct outlook in the past. We view this positively. Given the inventory rebuild (due to a prolonged correction), we see a rebound in general-purpose MCUs as customers replenish their warehouses. This is also confirmed by a book-to-bill ratio well above 1. These signal healthy demand conditions.

There are also positive tailwinds ahead. Beyond the cyclical recovery, several company-specific drivers are likely to contribute to earnings growth. For the first time, we have evidence to report. We see incremental top-line sales from key customers such as Apple and Tesla. That said, AI data centers and LEO satellites will combine to reach $500 billion and $1 billion in 2026 and 2027, respectively (Fig. 3). Even if we cannot price this development, the new Apple CEO might provide a tailwind for STM. In the past, he has overseen hardware across Apple's product portfolio, and we might expect new hardware features in each iPhone. Being STM's leading supplier for Cupertino, this is a plus to report.

Fig 3

On profitability, the Q2 gross margin came in ahead of Wall Street expectations. This was supported by 1) a more favorable product mix and 2) lower underutilization costs. The company sees further improvement in the year.

Lastly, STM announced a multi-year partnership with AWS. This multi-year, dollar commercial engagement will support the company's infrastructure and position STM as a critical supplier for the AI data center buildout. Key to the report is the NXP MEMS Sensor Acquisition. This was dated in early February and is an asset that STM has been looking to buy for a long time. This strategic acquisition will support the product portfolio in automotive safety and industrial sensors.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

There are many moving parts in assessing the STM 2026 financial forecast. On the top line, we increased our sales to €14.5 billion (from €13.6 billion), implying a sales pickup of almost 23% versus 2025 numbers. Regarding gross margin evolution, the company reported improved capacity absorption and cost discipline. Here at the Lab, we were already anticipating a higher gross margin at 35.7%. This is still aligned with STM's estimates. This results in an EBIT margin of 11% at €1.59 billion. Despite the acquisition, the company has a positive net cash position (Fig. 4), so we slightly increase profit before tax by €50 million, bringing it to €1.64 billion. Applying the STM tax rate (16%), we increase our net profit to €1.37 billion. In our calculation, we used a 16% tax rate, and considering the 911 million outstanding shares, STM's 2026 EPS is set at €1.51. Naturally, if we apply our P/E target of 20x, we should move STM to sell with a share price of €30. However, the company is in a recovery mood, with a 2027 EPS of €2.

At this stage, and somewhat departing from our usual fundamentals-based approach, we apply a 25x P/E multiple, in line with peers, to the average 2027 EPS estimates (which we believe will be revised upward over time). This results in a target price of €50 per share ($58.5 in ADR).

Fig 4

Risks

STM downside risks include continued underperformance in the Power & Discrete segment and higher-than-expected unused industrial capacity. We still believe the Automotive market will continue to suffer (and that Infineon is in a better position than STM in this segment, also thanks to a stronger product offering in China). Slower innovation and additional CAPEX are downside risks to consider. We positively report the latest inventory trends, but this remains an area of monitoring. A weakening dollar might not be playing any favor to STM earnings. Being a semis producer, STM needs to constantly improve its product portfolio, so any product failure has a multi-year commercial headwind. Compared to our last update, this analysis identifies multiple valuation risks that are already priced in, with an earnings recovery ahead.

Conclusion

STMicroelectronics has delivered an exceptionally strong start to the year. The Q1 performance shows a dramatic acceleration in sales. The latest acquisition and AWS partnerships support the company. We remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell's AI Moment: Why Inference And CPUs (Not GPUs), Is Driving The Next Growth Cycle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage of Dell Technologies (DELL), in which I remained bullish based on the company’s strong ISG growth and robust AI infrastructure demand despite the margin pressure from rising DRAM/NAND memory prices, the stock jumped over 60%.

In this article, I examine how Bank of America’s (BoA) report regarding raised sales for the server CPU industry due to inference from AI agentic models benefits DELL and could lead to a new growth cycle.

About risks, almost the same risks as in my previous article continue to exist, namely low profit margins, price pressure, and high competition in the CSG segment, as well as supply chain limitations.

ISG Segment Continues to Lead Dell’s Growth Story

The big picture is that the company reported 19% YoY growth in FY26, along with 31% operating income growth. Specifically, the AI-related Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) segment grew by 40% in FY26—with the AI-Optimized Servers Revenue business line increasing by 166% and the Traditional Servers & Networking Revenue business just 9%—while the PC-related segment, Client Solutions Group (CSG), grew only 5%, and operating income declined by 5%.

Also, the company disclosed $43B in AI backlog, with $34.1B booked in Q4’26, while the company stated that “for FY '27, (we) expect $50 billion in AI revenue, about 100% growth year-over-year,” reflecting that DELL continues to have strong momentum in the AI business.

The company runs under two completely different operating models, in terms of revenue and profit.

The ISG segment is DELL’s fastest-growing segment in terms of revenue; notably, only in Q4’26 it grew 73% YoY; however, due to its capital‑intensive nature, it has a low operating margin of about 11.7% in FY26, down compared to about 12.8% in the previous year. Similarly, the CSG segment holds an operating margin of 5.5% in FY26, compared to 6.14% in the previous year, but, as already mentioned, remains relatively flat on a full‑year basis, despite the 14% revenue growth in Q4. The decrease in CSG’s operating margin was a result of the company’s strategy not to increase prices in order to gain market share, despite the increasing prices of DRAM (5.5x increase in six months) and NAND (4x increase in six months) memory. That said, Dell’s business grew 18%, while the market grew 10%, meaning that Dell gained 100 basis points of share.

In contrast, specific business lines within both segments increase the company’s overall profitability, which means Dell’s profitability is highly sensitive to sales mix. Dell‑IP storage solutions and OpEx discipline, which was down 320 bps to 9.9% of revenue, boost the company’s profitability. If you want to learn more about the Dell‑IP business, refer to my previous coverage.

Investors need to know that, despite the massive demand for AI servers, the company does not anticipate any margin expansion in that segment. Especially, it is positioned as: “Profitability is in line with our mid-single-digit operating margin target. We like our position, the line of sight we have with our backlog and pipeline”.

Given that, as investors, Dell’s profitability remains a risk concern. OpEx reductions and the Dell‑IP business contribute to profitability, but both have limitations regarding how much they could contribute in the long term. I would like to see AI server products and the CSG segment achieve higher operating margins due to pricing power and provide a sustainable profitable business model, rather than OpEx and sales mix being the main margin drivers.

Overall, the company achieved net income growth of 39% YoY, along with a 5.23% profit margin in FY26, compared to about 4.79% in FY25

Inference Creates a New Cycle of Growth, CPUs Will Be the “Star”, and Not GPUs

Let me start with the company’s management statement:

Moving to traditional servers… We saw broad-based strength with units up, a larger active buyer base and a richer mix of our 16th and 17th generation platforms as customers shifted to dense high-performance configurations. The ROI to refresh is compelling. Even at higher ASPs, customers see a 7:1 consolidation when upgrading from the 14th generation to our latest platforms… Traditional x86 is benefiting from AI infrastructure build-outs. While many AI workloads rely on specialized GPUs, traditional compute remains essential for orchestration, data processing and inference support.

Combining the above statement with BoA’s report, which says:

We believe CPUs are increasingly becoming essential in sequential and latency-sensitive workloads, and are an integral part of overall AI infrastructure. The team expects CPUs to consistently make up an approximately 5% share of the overall AI data center total addressable market (TAM) of approximately $1.4 trillion. They estimate a +21% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) toward a TAM of $70+ billion by 2030, up from approximately $28 billion in 2025.

Now, take into account that Morgan Stanley said, also said that agentic AI systems could increase CPU demand by 2030, changing data center buildouts and spreading AI spending beyond GPUs

What do all the above mean?

In a single phrase, in my view, means a new growth cycle for the company!

Let me explain. The inference of AI agents and tools -not just chatbots like ChatGPT or Gemini- requires more CPU per GPU due to CPUs are responsible for managing orchestration and data handling. In simpler terms, CPUs are responsible for organizing the various steps that an AI agent needs to follow in order to provide an answer.

For this reason, today, as of April 24, 2026, Intel says that AI inference pushes the GPU/CPU ratio to change from 1:8 toward 1:1. For DELL, this means a new cycle of demand for new server configurations, especially designed for a 1:1 ratio. Remember that Dell does not sell CPUs but sells servers designed to include GPUs, CPUs, memory, cooling systems, and other staff—this is called rack systems. Moreover, the company stated that clients replace their older servers with the new 16th and 17th generation platforms, even if they are at higher prices.

Dell buys CPUs and GPUs from other companies, such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Intel (INTC), and could combine them with its branded memory, cooling systems, and software, and offer a full rack system to AI infrastructure companies.

Given all the above, I believe that the company has entered a new cycle of growth!

Valuation and Conclusion

Overall, the company seems undervalued compared to the sector, despite its YTD price return of 71%, reaching a record high price.

In terms of growth, the company is expected to achieve double‑digit revenue growth in the following years. In my view, Dell could achieve this growth rate for a longer period, given that the estimation below is prior to BoA’s and Morgan Stanley’s analysis. Therefore, a FWD P/S below 1 is more than attractive.

In terms of FWD P/E, Dell also has attractive metrics, given that it is entering a new growth cycle.

My main concern remains the company’s traditional segment, the CSG, which was almost flat in FY’26 and whose operating income decreased by 5% YoY. Overall, the segment seems weak, given that it is a highly cyclical segment and currently is in a down phase of the cycle.

Given the successful track record and the massive backlog, I am not concerned about an AI slowdown that could reduce the company’s growth trajectory, but I do see supply chain limitations and fluctuations as risks. The company stated:

Across the industry, the environment remains highly dynamic with unprecedented AI demand creating sustained supply tightness and frequent pricing resets.

This means that Dell may need to face even higher NAND/DRAM memory prices. Given that it has long-term agreements (LTAs) and capacity agreements with its suppliers, it helps to minimize supply chain risk, but it is still a key aspect to monitor. Also, Dell seems to have pricing power with price adjustments, as in servers it changed prices in mid-December, aiming to stabilize margins due to the higher memory costs.

In conclusion, Dell remains an undervalued stock, in my view, with impressive revenue growth and room for further expansion. Inference via AI agents could boost the company’s server business, but investors need to monitor its profitability and supply chain limitations.

Given all the above, I rerate the stock as BUY.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks Still Has Room To Run Despite A Rich Valuation
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) still looks strong heading into Q3 and beyond. The company keeps posting big numbers in Next-Generation Security (NGS), has made good moves with deals like CyberArk and Chronosphere, and keeps growing both recurring revenue and profit margins, with Q2 NGS ARR at $6.33 billion, up 33% year-over-year, and non-GAAP operating margin at 30.3%. I’m giving it a Buy rating here. The growth story doesn’t look shaky, and the upside seems worth it, even if the stock isn’t cheap. Expectations are already high, but management keeps hitting targets and signaling more gains ahead, so I think that’s fair.

Shares go for about 47 times expected earnings and about 12.5 times next year’s sales. Those are steep numbers for a cybersecurity company, but not totally out of line given how fast ARR is growing, how much cash the business throws off, and how much market share they keep taking. The company’s margins are also heading up, and there’s a clear path for that to continue. What stands out is that PANW keeps delivering both on sales and profits, and they’re actually making progress on those longer-term goals they keep talking about.

NGS Keeps Expanding

The NGS platform now covers more ground with CyberArk and Chronosphere on board. For Q3, they’re targeting $7.94 to $7.96 billion in NGS ARR, up 56% from last year. About $1.47 billion of that is coming from the new acquisitions, which is a big chunk, but it doesn’t take away from the fact that their NGS business was already growing fast. The numbers back that up: last quarter, NGS ARR hit $6.33 billion, up 33% year-over-year, including a $200 million contribution from Chronosphere; on an organic basis, NGS ARR was up 28% year-over-year. CyberArk is a big deal for them; identity security is getting more important, and as of December 2025, the CyberArk platform was pulling in over $1.2 billion in identity-related NGS ARR, up 30% from the year before. Management says, “Our Q3 and full year 2026 guidance assumes reported NGS ARR for CyberArk will be approximately 2% to 3% lower than the equivalent on the CyberArk previous bookings-based ARR definition,” so they’re being conservative about how fast they’ll actually convert those bookings into recurring revenue, which should help avoid any ugly surprises as they finish integrating everything.

Margins Keep Climbing

PANW’s main growth drivers are showing up in profits. In Q2, the company hit a 30.3% non-GAAP operating margin, up 190 basis points from last year. Over the past twelve months, free cash flow came in at $3.75 billion, with a margin of 37.9%. Management says they still want to reach a 40% free cash flow margin by FY28 and $20 billion in NGS ARR by 2030. They’re not just saying that for show; they keep tracking progress quarter by quarter, trying to make the most of integration and getting more out of headcount. From management: “Integration is being executed with the same rigor that we apply to running our core business… [with] clear governance… and measures to ensure continuity for customers, partners, and employees.” In other words, they’re keeping things steady. For Q3, they’re projecting revenue between $2.941 and $2.945 billion, up 28--29% year-over-year. Non-GAAP EPS is expected at $0.78 to $0.80. For the full year, they see revenue landing between $11.28 and $11.31 billion (22-23% up) and EPS at $3.65-$3.70. They expect to keep an operating margin of around 28.5-29% through FY26 and hold the free cash flow margin at 37%.

Acquisitions Add Pressure

Management isn’t pretending all this growth comes easy. Expanding the product lineup this fast, while facing about $4.9 billion in combined cash outlay across Chronosphere and CyberArk, with CyberArk closing after Q2 ended, brings real challenges. There’s a short-term hit to EPS from all the M&A. They acknowledge that but say the “focus is on a frictionless onboarding experience… to unlock the full value of these acquisitions.” The SASE platform is one of the clearest signs things are working; it’s now over $1.5 billion in ARR, up about 40% from last year. Cortex XSIAM crossed the $0.5 billion ARR mark, with more than 600 customers spending almost $1 million each in ARR. Both have pulled in new customers and managed to get more business from the existing base.

Cash Still Supports

The balance sheet looks strong. Free cash flow holds up, both in dollar terms and as a percent of revenue, which lets them keep buying back shares, including the new $1 billion authorization they announced in March 2026. At the end of Q2, they had $4.158 billion in cash and equivalents, or $7.898 billion including investments, and that figure already included the $2.6 billion cash outlay for Chronosphere. There’s another $2.3 billion earmarked for the CyberArk deal in Q3, but even after that, there’s still plenty of room to spend more if they want to move on to other deals or just stay flexible, assuming they don’t get sloppy with discipline.

Big picture, the industry’s still got a lot going for it. Companies keep moving toward bundled security platforms, AI-powered threat detection, and cloud-first tools. All of these trends look like they’ll keep boosting demand for several years. The company’s platform pitch is landing. Growth in SASE and AI-driven SOC products shows up in sales cycles. Analyst coverage mirrors this, with Benchmark initiating at Buy, Wells Fargo maintaining Overweight, and Citi calling the post-earnings reaction nothing but a “short-term speed bump.” There’s also a premium building in the sector for companies with a shot at leading in AI and quantum security. Both analysts and the company itself keep pointing to new launches, Prisma AIRS, a secure browser, and quantum-ready certificate management as proof that interest and adoption aren’t fading.

Risks

Pulling off integrations at this size isn’t simple. If they mess up with either product or sales alignment for CyberArk or Chronosphere, that could break their growth path and force investors to rethink the stock. They depend on big distributors; three of them handle 42% of all revenue and nearly half of what’s owed to them as of January 2026. If any of those relationships weaken, or if incentives get out of sync, they could face real trouble. Price competition is always lurking. The company even says, “The sales prices of our products, subscriptions, and support offerings may decline for a variety of reasons, including competitive pricing pressures…” That would hit their margins if they can’t keep innovating or cutting costs. Broader economic stuff matters too. If overseas business hits snags, especially in a bad economy or if politics get tense, then working capital slows down, and short-term cash flow and operating results can come under pressure.

Valuation Needs Execution

The stock trades at a pretty steep premium. Still, the price doesn’t assume everything will go perfectly. If they hit the top end of their goals for fiscal 2026, $11.31 billion in revenue, $3.70 EPS, and can push free cash flow margins to at least 40% by FY28, then the stock should be able to hold onto its current earnings multiple, maybe even see it go higher, as long as NGS ARR and overall margins don’t suddenly slow down.

Next, I’m watching a few things to see if the story holds up: NGS ARR each quarter (the target for FY26 is $8.52-$8.62 billion, which includes $1.52 billion from M&A), operating margins moving toward the FY26 range, free cash flow margin moving toward the FY28 goal, and whether SASE, Cortex, and Identity segments keep growing as they bring everything together. How well they bring in and grow the CyberArk and Chronosphere businesses will show up in retention, cross-sell, and ARR; they’ll need to prove those deals are working. I think the combined platform can still bring in more ARR as PANW pushes Prisma AIRS, the secure browser, and quantum-ready certificate management. That’s backed up by what they’re saying on recent earnings calls.

Bull Case Holds

Big picture, PANW is combining strong growth, profitability, and product breadth, with Q2 NGS ARR up 33% year-over-year and non-GAAP operating margin at 30.3%. The bull case doesn’t need any crazy assumptions, just solid execution, pulling together all the pieces, and taking more market share, which seems to be happening. The path to $20 billion NGS ARR by 2030 and 40% free cash flow margins by FY28 isn’t easy, but with every quarter, it looks more doable. Most of the risk is still skewed to the upside, and that’s supported by both the fundamentals and what’s happening in the sector. If they start having real trouble pulling everything together, margins keep shrinking, or NGS ARR falls short, I’ll change my mind. For now, the odds look pretty good for the bulls.

data.title: PHK: NAV Trending Slightly Higher But Still Not A Buy
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK), I issued a hold rating due to the flat earnings and questionable dividend coverage at the time. Since then, the fund's share price has declined following continued headwinds in the debt markets. High interest rates continue to put pressure on credit funds and increase their risk profile. Since my last coverage, PHK has released an updated semi-annual report that I wanted to use to reassess the fund's earnings, performance, and valuation.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PHK's share price has only slightly recovered from the initial decline related to the deterioration of the debt markets. Meanwhile, PHK's total return jumps up to 12.6% over the same time frame. PHK now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 12.3% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. Dividend coverage can certainly improve going forward, but I anticipate that PHK is likely to keep payouts consistent for the remainder of the year.

Unfortunately, PHK's success seems to be reliant on a lower interest rate environment. Although the NAV has steadily climbed higher over the last few years, I don't expect there to be any meaningful NAV growth until interest rates are lowered. A lower cost of debt would encourage more borrowing activity and higher valuations for PHK's portfolio. The challenge is that we don't know how much longer interest rates will remain elevated. Until interest rates are lowered, the dividend coverage is likely to also remain thin, which can threaten the appeal for income investors seeking safety from the equity markets.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PHK has total managed assets of $1.1B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund's primary goal is to generate a high level of income, so the success of PHK is not correlated to the movement of the equity markets. Instead, PHK's success is reliant on borrowers' ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance over time. In order to get the high yield it needs to support distributions, PHK allocates portions of its portfolio to low-rated securities and some global positions. For instance, the breakdown by region is as follows:

  • United States: 63.33%
  • Bermuda: 3.41%
  • France: 3.30%
  • United Kingdom: 3.02%
  • Colombia: 2.74%

Additionally, the fund also maintains diversity across different industries. PHK is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 7.83% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to healthcare and wireless investments, which accounts for 4.41% and 3.93% of assets, respectively. However, I think it's worth mentioning that PHK's larger allocation to technology securities may be a source of risk during a time period where the markets continue to punish software companies. While there's limited visibility into how much of PHK's technology investments are specifically exposed to software, it is still something to consider if you believe AI will continue to drive the software sector lower.

PHK also tends to lean more heavily on short-dated securities that have maturities of less than five years out. Shorter-dated securities tend to offer more resilience to fluctuations in interest rates, but it also means that management has to actively work harder to replace these securities once they reach maturity. Under a high interest rate environment, replacing these short-dated securities can be difficult. Conversely, longer-dated maturities tend to be more sensitive to high interest rates but can offer a secure source of income within the portfolio for a longer period of time.

Updated Performance Metrics

When I previously covered PHK, the fund traded at a premium to NAV of 6.22%. Following the recent decline in share price, PHK now trades at a slightly lower premium to NAV of 2.64%. While this may not seem like the most attractive valuation, we have to consider that the price still trades below PHK's typical range. Referring to the red line below, we can see that this is actually near the lower end of PHK's historical price-to-NAV range. For instance, the fund has traded at an average premium to NAV of 6.5% over the last five years.

Since my last coverage, the fund has also released an updated semi-annual report that provides us with some transparency into its earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.24 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.09 per share. Assuming that PHK is capable of achieving similar results through the second half of this period, the fund is on track to generate net investment income that falls within its typical historical range. This is a great indication that the underlying portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income through changing market conditions.

However, I dislike the fact that PHK's net investment income doesn't support the distribution in its entirety. While this isn't necessarily a big deal, it does introduce a vulnerability to market downturns. Since PHK does not generate sufficient net investment income by itself, the fund has to depend on net realized gains to bridge any gaps in earnings. This is a common dynamic for high-yield closed-end funds, but it does mean that it puts PHK at the risk of paying out more than it earns.

If a fund is frequently paying out more than it earns, the underlying NAV will erode over time. This is usually only a big deal during periods of market declines. A good example of this was demonstrated through 2022's market decline, which resulted in PHK reporting large net realized losses of $1.14 per share. This large net realized loss resulted in PHK's NAV declining, which the fund still hasn't fully recovered from. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $5.01 per share
  • 2021: $5.92 per share
  • 2022: $4.72 per share
  • 2023: $4.51 per share
  • 2024: $4.56 per share
  • 2025: $4.62 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $4.67 per share

As we can see, PHK's NAV has failed to maintain enough positive momentum to get back to its high of 2021. On the bright side, PHK has been able to steadily increase its NAV over the last few years. As long as we don't see any further headwinds in the debt markets, such as an increase in interest rates, PHK's growth may continue.

Outlook Risks

Despite the fund's short-term improvement of its NAV growth, the long-term performance makes it clear that there are some structural flaws that need to be improved. For instance, PHK's share price started its decline long before the volatility of the pandemic. Over the last ten years, PHK's share price declined by roughly 49.7%, despite market indices racing to all-time highs over the last decade. When including all distributions paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to 53.5% over the same time frame. Although the total return is positive, I don't believe it justifies the erosion of capital.

However, the main contributor to PHK's suppressed share price since 2022 is the high interest rate environment. Since PHK's positions tend to share an inverse relation with interest rates, the value of its holdings declined as soon as the Fed hiked interest rates to new highs over the last ten years. During the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. I believe there are two ways that high interest rates will continue to be a headwind for PHK:

  1. Limited growth opportunities for management to invest in.
  2. Elevated probability of borrower defaults will keep the market pessimistic.

Since PHK leans most heavily on high-yield credit investments, underlying earnings growth relies on management's ability to allocate capital towards new borrowers. However, the simple truth is that a higher cost of debt limits the number of borrowers in the market seeking debt financing. A high cost of debt causes borrowers to wait until interest rates fall so that their interest expenses will be less of a burden.

The second risk going forward would be the exposure to borrower defaults. Even if PHK's portfolio doesn't specifically go through any defaults, PHK's share price can be negatively impacted by the overall sentiment. After all, PHK does have some exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB tend to be riskier. These are borrowers that are more reliant on debt to support operations or have less consistent cash flows. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 58.89%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 34.14%
  • Not Rated: 6.98%

Looking forward, I believe the greatest catalyst for PHK would be lower interest rates. A lower cost of debt would create a more favorable borrowing environment and may cause an influx of new opportunities in the market. Furthermore, the value of its positions will increase, and PHK's spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities will improve.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.048 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.3%. Based on the fund's payout history over the last few years, it's safe to assume that PHK will likely keep payouts unchanged for the remainder of the year. This would result in PHK issuing an annual payout totaling $0.576 per share. Unfortunately, the fund has a very mixed history for income investors. Once again, payouts were already trending lower before the volatile market conditions caused by the pandemic.

I would actually argue that a dividend cut would be beneficial for the fund's long-term success. With a starting yield of 12.3%, PHK can implement a 20% dividend reduction. This would leave PHK with a starting dividend yield somewhere around 9.8%, which is still highly competitive among the high yield landscape. A lower payout would also improve the fund's spread by reducing the pressure of generating a high level of income. A lower payout can lead to excess cash flow for PHK, which could then be reinvested back into new growth opportunities and improve future earnings.

However, I believe that payouts should be able to be sustained going forward when referring to the latest UNII report. Based on the report, which is timed from March 2025 to March 2026, we can see that PHK has a dividend coverage rate of 92.3%. Although the fund doesn't completely support all of its distributions with earnings, PKH is actually one of the funds with a stronger distribution coverage rate above the 90% market. I attribute any gaps in coverage to the unfavorable market environment of higher interest rates, which is limited growth potential.

Lastly, investors should be aware that distributions received from PHK may not offer tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 81.49% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as net investment income, which has the highest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, I believe that PHK is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that PHK is still a hold at this time. Despite the fund's price-to-NAV valuation becoming a bit more attractive, I believe that PHK's growth potential is quite limited as long as interest rates remain elevated. The NAV has experienced a slow uptick over the last few years, but the portfolio structure reveals that PHK is reliant on net realized gains to drive growth over time. So until interest rates are substantially lowered, I don't believe that PHK offers a meaningful value proposition at this time. Furthermore, the dividend coverage remains tight, so if defaults occur, net investment income is likely to fall and weaken payouts.

data.title: Apollo Commercial: Business Model Is Changing Following Sale Of Loan Portfolio (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

It has now been more than two years since I covered Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance (ARI), so I thought it would be appropriate to revisit the fund and provide some updated insights around its performance, outlook, and current value proposition. When I previously covered ARI, I issued a buy rating because it appeared that the stock was unfairly punished as a result of higher interest rates. Since then, ARI's share price has only increased by a little more than 8%, but the total return is much higher at more than 48% because of dividends paid out to investors.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ARI's share price has increased by about 22%. Despite market indices having a rough start to 2026, ARI continues to demonstrate resilience. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps up above 34% for the same time frame. ARI now offers investors a high starting dividend yield of 9%.

However, the outlook of this high-yield stock has drastically shifted. The business has completed the sale of its loan portfolio, which was the main income engine. Following the sale of its loan portfolio, the outlook of how ARI will generate income seems like an uncertainty at this point. So I have downgraded my rating to a hold because of the questionable outlook. However, the transition isn't necessarily a bad thing for existing shareholders. ARI will now sit on a ton of cash, and with a reduced portfolio, the share price is likely to remain stable through the remainder of 2026.

The Old Portfolio Strategy

I will keep this brief since this no longer applies to ARI's portfolio strategy and means of generating income. According to the latest fund overview, ARI had a total investment value of $8.8B that was spread across 56 different loans. ARI operates within the global commercial real estate debt market, which means that it acts as the originator and manager of different loans. ARI's loans are typically secured by a form of collateral.

  1. 99% of loans are first mortgages.
  2. 96% of the portfolio is structured as floating-rate investments.

The first mortgage structure ensures that ARI holds a priority claim on the underlying real estate collateral. So in a scenario where one of ARI's borrowers goes through a default, ARI doesn't lose all invested capital. Additionally, the focus on floating rate positions means that ARI is capable of collecting a higher level of interest income during periods where interest rates are elevated. However, this all changed recently, and the outlook for the fund will look a lot different.

Uncertain Outlook Post Sale

During the last quarter, management highlighted that they have entered an agreement with Athene Holding to sell their entire loan portfolio. That sale has been finalized for $9B, meaning that ARI's only assets are now the real estate that it owns. Management can effectively use the proceeds from this sale and pay off its own debts and financing facilities. During a time when the debt market is being punished because of high-interest rates and concerns around defaults, this was a great move that helped reduce ARI's overall risk profile. This is likely one of the reasons why ARI's share price has held up so strong on a YTD basis.

What I like about ARI is that the current structure means that its REO (Real Estate Owned) segment can expand over time following this sale. When ARI takes control of one of its collateral properties, it then becomes an active operator instead of only being a lender. While this portion of ARI's portfolio is relatively small, it still demonstrates how it can be profitable over time. For instance, the benefit for the REO segment is displayed from the Brooklyn Multifamily Tower because ARI holds a meaningful level of equity here. This segment can also increase cash flows by implementing value-add renovations, cutting costs, and increasing occupancy over time.

ARI reported its Q4 earnings at the start of February. ARI reported diluted distributable earnings per share of $0.26, which was a decline from the prior year's total of $0.32 per share. Looking at the financials, it appears that the largest driver in the earnings decline was the higher operating expenses and currency related costs. For instance, ARI reported total operating expenses of $43.4M for the quarter, which was attributed to higher operating expenses related to its owned real estate. This was an increase from the prior year's total operating expenses of $38.4M.

Similarly, ARI saw a decline in total net revenue because of a decline in interest income collected from commercial mortgage loans. For ARI's fiscal year 2025, total net revenue landed at $271.5M, which was a reduction from the prior year's total net revenue of $303.6M. I would attribute this to the slightly lower interest rate environment compared to last year.

When considering the dynamics of its portfolio, the sale of its loan portfolio is a good thing. We don't know how much longer interest rates can remain high, so there was always the risk that total income would maintain a downward trend for ARI. Despite several interest rate cuts being implemented since the time of my last coverage, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to keep interest rates unchanged.

Uncertain Pivot

So now that ARI has eliminated its loan portfolio, the question is how they will continue to generate income. The sale of its portfolio means that it is difficult to estimate where the stock will be trading twelve months from now. The outlook ultimately depends on what management does with the cash pile going forward. After paying down debts and financing facilities, ARI will likely have a cash pile of around ~$2.2B. Through the remainder of the year, I anticipate that there are three ways for ARI to continue generating income for existing shareholders:

  1. ARI will continue expanding on its real estate owned assets.
  2. Collect low-risk interest income on the cash balance.
  3. Continue reducing costs within its existing business.

On the last earnings call, management confirmed that they will be putting effort into expanding on the revenues collected from its Brooklyn property. Additionally, ARI will likely implement value-add renovations to its other properties in Atlanta and D.C.

We're leasing depending on the month, 20 to 40 units a month. Rents are where we expected them to be. And as I indicated in my comments, I think we'll hit stabilization the latter part of this year. At that point, it really becomes sort of an assessment of what does the market look like in terms of the transaction environment, the interest rate environment, etc., as we think about maximizing value on the Brook. - Stuart Rothstein, President, CEO & Director

However, it also appears that management isn't entirely sure which direction they plan to go in the future. My immediate thought is that management may end up reinventing their approach to focus on real estate properties or a complete dissolution. After the sale of its loan portfolio, I doubt management would take this capital and go back into the business of loans. So this is an interesting dynamic where it's a 'let's wait and see' scenario for investors.

I would say we're exactly where we thought we would be, which is I would say there are some specific ideas that have germinated organically internally that we are evaluating, but I would say too early to conclude whether one of those ideas will ultimately be what we decide to pursue or not. And then not surprisingly, post the announcement, a lot of incoming phone calls around ideas that people would like to propose to us, which was very much expected, and we will very much engage in a number of dialogues just to hear people out on what other thoughts they may have. - Stuart Rothstein, President, CEO & Director

The good news is that this means ARI's share price is likely to remain stable for the remainder of 2026, at least until future plans are officially established. Additionally, management decided to reduce its management fee by 50% to ensure that investors aren't paying full price for this period of uncertainty. However, I actually respect this decision to think things through because it prevents management from rushing into another parallel strategy while interest rates remain high.

Outlook: So What's Next?

ARI is actually set to report its Q1 2026 earnings at the end of April. But with the portfolio strategy shifting from reliance on loans, it now depends on how efficiently management can deploy the capital it now holds. So while management already confirmed that there aren't any definitive plans established yet, I believe there are still three aspects of the business that we should pay attention to:

  1. The burn rate of ARI's cash balance.
  2. Whether or not the REO portfolio actually scales through 2026.
  3. A future timeline of new ideas for the capital.

The most obvious metric would be to measure how much interest income the cash may be generating, assuming it is allocated to some sort of money market fund or U.S. treasuries. If the quarterly dividend is mostly made up of return of capital, this would be an indication that the existing REO assets aren't generating a sizeable amount of income.

Since the largest portion of the REO portfolio is the Brooklyn property, I will be looking to see what updates management provides. I hope to see that occupancy increases, increased leasing activity, or there is some sort of stabilization by the end of the year. Lastly, I hope that management is capable of providing a roadmap of ideas or for what their plans will be going forward.

So when it comes to the valuation of ARI, it is very difficult to determine right now. ARI has historically traded at a discounted price-to-book ratio between 0.75x and 0.85x, but the large cash balance provides a new floor. So if you were to initiate a position in ARI today, you'd essentially be buying a large cash pile with a few buildings at a discount. However, there just aren't any clear growth catalysts yet, so valuation or growth expectations can't accurately be measured.

However, the elimination of the loan portfolio means that I do anticipate the earnings to decline. ARI still holds $441M in net REO equity, but it wouldn't be realistic to expect this to make up for the elimination of the loan portfolio. The REO assets are likely more capital intensive since they require operating overhead like insurance, maintenance, and taxes. So distributable earnings per share are likely to fall below the dividend level for the remainder of 2026. This means that the dividend payout ratio will technically exceed 100% since ARI would be using the cash pile to pay those distributions instead.

Getting Paid To Wait

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.25 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9%. Although the portfolio is no longer generating income the way it traditionally has in the past, this high yield still serves as an incentive for existing shareholders to maintain a position. The high yield of 9% is essentially paying shareholders to wait for management's updates in the future. So although ARI has an inconsistent dividend history over the last few years, I think it's fair to estimate that distributions will remain at the current $0.25 per share level for the rest of the year.

Management also indicated that if no new strategy was finalized in the near-term, distributions received may be classified as return of capital. While these distributions will come directly from ARI's net assets and reduce its share price, it offers some tax efficiency for investors that utilize a position within a regular taxable account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that the responsible thing would be to downgrade my rating to a hold at this time. ARI's structural shift means that the outlook is uncertain. The sale of its loan portfolio leaves ARI with its real estate-owned assets, which means that income generation is reduced. Following the sale, ARI will sit on a large cash pile that can also generate some low-risk interest income, but management's plans are a bit uncertain at this time. Existing shareholders can remain invested and collect a high yield in the meantime, so they are essentially getting paid to wait and see what happens. However, there's no compelling reason for brand-new investors to initiate a position at this time before any new plans are made.

data.title: Trump Media: Sell It And Forget It
data.description:
I wanted to go over Trump Media & Technology Group (DJT) since it released its FY 25 numbers recently and see if anything changed for the positive. In short, core operations are stagnating, the company is losing even more cash to keep it running, and it will likely soon lose its identity.

2025 Performance

Starting from the top, the net sales number has not moved a whole lot compared to last year, coming in at 1.7% higher than FY24 at $3.68m. So, no growth. Unfortunately, the company keeps avoiding the use of key performance metrics like ARPU, MAU, and DAU, but what we can gather from this lack of growth is that none of these have changed. It seems that Truth Social is monetizing the same people as it had in the past, and they are not doing a particularly good job. Also, the management doesn't do any conference calls, so we don't get any information on where the company is heading. All the shareholders get is a small statement at the end of the year about what the company has and vague growth strategies in the form of a few PDF pages, such as this one.

Let's move on to the company's profitability, or the lack of it. So, the company made over $3m in sales, but its cost of sales went from around $600k last year to over $1.6m, so its gross margins went from 82%, which was very good, down to 54%. Not good. But that's not the worst part. DJT keeps spending over $40m in R&D and over $119m in G&A expenses, so that is already bringing the company further in the red than before. What's even worse is that DJT also realized $403m in losses on its digital losses, which is its Cronos and Bitcoin, bringing its net loss for the year to over $712m after interest expenses and additional investment losses of $200m or so.

So, losing $700m on paper is a lot; let's see how the company is going to support this going forward. Cash and equivalents came in at around $134m, ST investments at around $305m, and the company essentially had no long -term debt in comparison. Only around $5m, so that will help it survive a little while longer. The company also has around $722m in equity securities, which are its digital assets I mentioned earlier. They could easily sell these on the open market and receive the proceeds; however, seeing that the company is down $400m on these assets, as we saw above, I don't think they will sell them. Nevertheless, DJT has plenty of liquidity, and the fact that it has essentially no debt means it will continue to operate and burn through its capital.

Looking at the company's cash flow statement, the management was proud that its cash from operations was positive this year, at around $14.7m. It is a decent improvement for sure compared to last year's cash outflows of around $61m, but I don't think this is going to be sustainable. The management also highlighted that it got $44m in cash proceeds from its derivative options plays. The company uses options, which I tend to do from time to time as well, as another source of income on either my cash or my stock ownership as collateral. Here, the company said that it made cash-secured puts, or covered calls, but essentially, what this meant is that they were writing options for premiums that they get upfront. It got $44m in premiums, which is an immediate cash flow because you get it as soon as you write the option.

What this doesn't tell us is the fact that its overall equity investment was still down. We can see that reflected in the company's income statement above, which states that the value of its investments reduced by $400m. So, what these options play really means is that they helped offset around $44m in losses, if they end up selling their position at this time, which is a loss. It is smarter to be a writer of options. I agree with that strategy, but that doesn't mean there is no risk. Short options carry unlimited risk if uncapped.

So, if you sell a call contract thinking that BTC or a company's share price won't rise above it, and it does, you can, in theory, rack up infinite losses because the share price can rise to infinity. On the other side, selling a put is also a good strategy because it says if it goes to that strike price, you have to buy the asset at that price, but what happens if the asset continues to drop and the options expire in the money? You have an obligation to buy at, let's say, $100 from someone who exercised it, but the stock price on the open market is already at $50, so you immediately realize a $50 loss. What I'm trying to say here is that derivatives are risky, and sure, it worked out once, but it may not another time, and also, the premium received may not offset the loss in its equity position, even if it's an unrealized loss.

Overall, I don't see any improvement in the company's operations. To me, it seems like the echo chamber of Truth Social hasn't grown at all, and the company was not able to monetize its stagnant user base more efficiently. It has great liquidity and no risk of default since it has very little debt; however, if there is no revenue, the company will never climb out of the loss-making operations and will eat up its capital to keep going for a few more years. The derivative options strategy only works when BTC or other equity securities don't break through the sold strike price to collect 100% of the premium at expiry, but it only offsets the losses that the collateral experienced.

Comments on the Outlook

Truth Social

Trump's social media platform is practically the whole operation of DJT. It is its core operating product, so the fact that it is looking to spin off businesses, including Truth Social, just sounds like everyone is jumping ship and is hoping to start another business to pump numbers in the beginning, get money from gullible investors, and repeat the process after the business stagnates again. So, what is DJT going to be left with if the spin-offs happen? Non-Truth Social assets and any businesses that are not distributed to shareholders. That's the short answer. The longer version is that the company keeps trying to change its identity, and the latest one that DJT is going to try once Truth Social is spun off is its venture into fusion energy, as well as keeping its ventures in cryptocurrency accumulation and other financial products.

None of this is set in stone yet, which means the company may yet again pivot to something else or not do anything at all. I would argue that the cryptocurrency treasury will be one of the main operations going forward since the company has a lot of assets there.

Prediction Markets

Another thing that the management is trying to get into is the hot new cash cow of prediction markets that seems to be taking over the world, or at least the US. It is essentially the same as gambling on sports, but it is not covered by the gambling tax law, as it is governed by the CFTC, which means a lot fewer taxes to be paid by these companies, who are adamant that what they offer are derivative contracts and not gambling odds. I have to commend the proactivity of the management to try a lot of strategies and see which one sticks. Prediction markets seem to be quite promising, and if it goes live, I believe the company will manage to monetize its stagnant user base further or even attract more users, just because they want to "not gamble" on political events and other things, but it will be mostly sports.

Closing Comments

The company is a mess, to put it lightly. It has very little identity of what it is, namely its Truth Social platform, and with that being spun off in the near future, it will have no identity at all, and I don't see why anyone would want to buy this, if not for a quick buck in hopes that Trump will say something one day that will pump the stock and then you sell it right after.

This is not a sound investment. Fundamentals are broken. Operations as they are will never support their growth, and their cash reserves and crypto investments may continue to dwindle. There are better high-risk plays out there that will have much better long-term outcomes. If you want social media exposure, buy Meta (META), and Reddit (RDDT). If you want prediction markets, buy Robinhood (HOOD), Flutter Entertainment (FLUT), or FanDuel (DUEL). If you want crypto exposure, just buy crypto yourself; you will most likely pay less than a company that accumulates crypto.

Sell whatever you've got left in DJT and never look back.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: Fundamentals Are Solid, But The Price Is Too High - Downgrading From Buy To Hold
data.description:
I have been a long-standing Procter & Gamble (PG) bull, but today I believe it is time to downgrade from buy to hold.

I started covering the firm back in 2022, and I maintained a bullish rating ever since, due to the firm's relatively defensive nature, as well as its strong track record of dividend payments. I have to realize, however, that the macroeconomic headwinds are currently even stronger than before, and the macro environment is highly uncertain - but the valuation does not seem to be reflective of these difficulties ahead. My goal today is to give a balanced view of the firm based on the most recent quarterly results, and highlight the key reasons, why I believe a hold rating is more justifiable.

1. High demand for PG's products globally

I would like to start my writing on a positive note and emphasize that PG's business is still very robust. Despite the poor consumer confidence levels, PG managed to achieve broad-based growth, with organic sales up as much as 3%, driven by both volume and pricing. This growth was also well-diversified geographically, with each region and each product category growing. There are two things I find important here:

1. The growth is organic with volume up, meaning that the demand for the products is strong.

2. The growth is broad-based, meaning that there is no single region or product category that the firm is heavily relying on - which is a plus, in my view.

Let us break down the results by segment, and let me highlight a couple of key points I believe are important to pay attention to. For the beauty segment, while organic sales and volumes both grew, increased commodity prices already created headwinds. I do not believe that these headwinds are going to ease in the coming months. Also, skin care and hair care shares declined, a potential result of the poor sentiment levels, which I also do not believe will improve in the near term.

For the grooming segment, pricing was the main driver of growth, with volume down 2%. This segment, therefore, seems more price sensitive, and the demand appears to be more elastic. If consumer confidence were to remain low, I believe this segment is likely to perform worse in the coming months.

In the health care segment, the firm managed to gain shares, both in oral care and in personal health care, but the organic volume still declined. This segment also appears price sensitive with demand elasticity.

Fabric and home care performed well, however, with growth driven by both volume and pricing - indicating less demand elasticity. The firm also managed to gain shares in the home care segment. Commodity headwinds are also likely to keep negatively impacting this segment, just like they did in the most recent quarter. In my view, elevated input costs could have a material impact on profitability here.

Last, but not least, baby, feminine and family care. Increased volume and a flat price mean that the customers in this segment are also less price sensitive. The success of this segment was relying on lower commodity costs, which I believe is not going to last going forward.

All in all, I still find PG's business fundamentally attractive. They achieved robust organic sales growth in each category, and in many categories both volume and pricing were up. But the question is what to expect going forward, and how much should we pay for this business?

2. Guidance

Continuing on a positive note, I also have to say that I find the firm's guidance attractive, in light of the macro circumstances. Sales, EPS, and adjusted free cash flow productivity are all set to increase or at least stay flat, according to the lower end of their estimates. Of course, when it comes to the attractiveness of these figures, it is not only about how it shapes up operationally, but it is also about, how much an investor should pay for such an outlook - but more on this in the valuation section.

3. Capital structure

While I was satisfied with PG's most recent quarterly results, it is time to raise my concerns about why I believe that a downgrade is warranted. First, I would like to talk about the capital structure, specifically about the debt. The firm has a total debt of $36.6 billion, in contrast to the $14.9 billion cash.

When it comes to liquidity, the firm is also not very well positioned, as both their current and quick ratios are below 1, meaning that the current assets are less than the current liabilities.

SO why is it actually problematic? I appreciate that PG's business is capital intensive and they do need substantial debt to run the business. But when interest rates are high, this becomes a material risk. And since the start of the war in Iran, the uncertainty around the level of interest rates jumped significantly.

How are these even connected? Well, when the war broke out, the Strait of Hormuz was closed, preventing oil from Gulf countries from reaching the market, leading to a surge in the oil prices globally. In the past few weeks, after a ceasefire was agreed upon, oil prices moderated somewhat, but a peace deal that could end the war and lead to the opening of the strait still seems to be far away, despite the U.S. delegation just heading over to Pakistan for direct talks with Iran. As a result, oil prices spiked again.

And as long as energy prices are high, transportation and manufacturing expenses are also going to be higher, creating inflationary pressures. One of the tools of the Fed to combat the inflationary pressures is to keep interest rates higher for longer. We will have to watch the upcoming FOMC meeting in 4 days to gauge the tone, and see how the interest rate environment might change in the coming months and quarters, as this could have a material impact not only on P&G but on most firms.

All in all, higher debt in an uncertain interest rate landscape creates additional risk, which I believe makes PG's stock unattractive right now. Or at least not at the current valuation. But, as I have mentioned valuation many times now, let us move on to the next section.

4. Dividends and Valuation

I have been praising PG for its long-standing dividend payments, which grew each year in the past 69 years. For this reason, I am going to be using a dividend discount model to assess the company's valuation today.

The key input parameters needed are dividend growth in the future and the required rate of return. To come up with reasonable growth figures, let us look at the firm's historic growth rates. For the next 5 years, I am going to be using a growth rate of 6%, equivalent to the 5-year CAGR historically, and afterwards, I will assume a 2.5% growth, which is roughly equivalent to the growth of the economy overall.

For the required rate of return, I will use 7.55%, equivalent to the company's cost of equity.

These inputs yield a fair value of $111 per share, significantly below the current share price.

To come closer to the current share price, I would need to assume 6% growth for the next 5 years, and 4% in perpetuity.

In my view, even the first assumption is too aggressive. Considering the macro landscape, the firm's organic growth, and the EPS growth estimates, I believe that achieving a 6% dividend growth for the next five years is not going to be easy. For the second case, 4% growth in perpetuity is very aggressive in my view. For these reasons, I believe that the macroeconomic headwinds are not currently priced in properly and there is significant downside risk from the current levels. A similar kind of overvaluation can be observed also based on a set of price multiples if we compared PG's metrics to the sector median.

All in all, I believe that considering the fundamentals and the macroeconomic landscape, PG is overvalued, and at these levels a buy rating can no longer be justified.

Conclusions

I find PG's robust organic sales growth attractive, but not attractive enough to maintain my buy rating. High levels of debt, commodity headwinds, and low consumer confidence are also raising concerns, and they do not seem to be priced in.

Based on dividend discount models, there is significant downside potential, even with relatively aggressive assumptions.

For these reasons, I downgrade from buy to hold.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Earnings Set To Reveal Massive Rewards
data.description:
Oil prices have been all over the headlines for most investors around the world because oil prices impact everything else. If we think about it, oil is largely foundational for much in our modern society. From plastics, gasoline, and lubrication to everything in between. Oil plays a pivotal role, whether it's supplying the energy and power, the heat, or the base feedstock for many different items.

So when oil prices see massive levels of volatility, the question many investors ask themselves is, how do we benefit from this environment? Especially if your wallet is going to get hurt in so many other avenues of life, how can we benefit from the oil volatility in your portfolio?

Today and tomorrow, I plan on covering two outstanding commodity-focused investments that benefit from this increased oil pricing volatility. When it all settles down, the dust is settled, the Iran war is over, and oil prices come back down to where they were previously, these companies are set to reward their shareholders massively, unlike many of their peers.

Let's dive in!

Northern Oil Producer That's Safe With A Low Breakeven Price

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) is primarily a Canadian-based and Canadian-focused oil and natural gas producer.

CNQ does have assets in Africa and Europe, but the lion's share of their revenue is generated by their Canadian assets.

Being a Canadian O&G producer has not been easy for most of 2026. Originally, oil prices were expected to be trending downward this year. When we covered CNQ in December, we wrote.

The other primary risk to CNQ is geopolitical. As with any oil and gas producer, the price of oil can heavily impact their earnings. Oil prices are expected to be lower in 2026 than they were in 2025. Even though demand is rising, there is a greater supply in the market, and that supply has to be absorbed before prices are expected to recover. CNQ uses West Texas Intermediate, WTI, crude oil prices to measure its break-even price. The break-even price is the price where CNQ turns a profit on its oil production. According to CNQ, in March of 2025, their break-even price was low to mid $40 per barrel. Right now, it's expected for WTI to be around the $51-52 range per barrel in 2026. This leaves them room for their prices to fall further and CNQ to still be operating at a profit. Growth for CNQ will need to focus on a lower break-even point for them to find added profitability throughout next year.

CNQ struggled heavily due to the United States military operation in Venezuela. This led to a strong sell-off to start 2026.

Oil prices in the United States dropped in the belief that cheap heavy oil from Venezuela was going to negatively impact oil prices by adding more supply to a market awash with oil. We took time to review the impact of Venezuelan oil on the overall oil supply market and determined that, in the short term, it wasn't going to be a big issue for CNQ. We wrote a review of CNQ in light of the new potential oil paradigm.

CNQ estimated that they are able to break even if oil prices within the United States were to fall to $40 a barrel. At this point in time, prices are still well above that and are not expected to fall below $40 a barrel in 2026, even factoring in Venezuelan crude. CNQ remains an attractive investment and is worth adding to. This unexpected geopolitical event only drives it further into deep value territory. Many are pre-selling on the fear of oil production that hasn't occurred and won't be able to occur in the near-term.

As many of you know, without needing to be reminded, there was a lot of panic. There was also a new brewing situation. It wasn't many months later that the United States engaged in warfare with Iran. This quickly led to the Strait of Hormuz closing, preventing 25% of global seaborne oil from safely passing through a pivotal chokepoint. Oil prices rebounded and even hit triple digits, over double what many predicted oil would be trading for in 2026 because of concerns of oversupply. Now there was a concern of a lack of supply.

Oil prices are significantly higher than where they were at the beginning of the year and well above the forecasted price of West Texas Intermediate crude being $52 a barrel in 2026, as we mentioned in December of 2025. CNQ has a breakeven price in the $40 range, meaning that any dollar that oil trades above that is profitable for them, and the higher it goes, the bigger their profit goes.

CNQ has a relatively balanced approach to its overall production, with a strong level of production coming from oil and a strong level of production coming from natural gas. Natural gas prices really haven't been heavily impacted by the recent military action, but to put into perspective how much revenue CNQ generates from each of those businesses, we can understand why oil prices have a greater impact on them.

CNQ generated $19 billion CAD in Oil and Natural Gas Liquids (NGL) revenue compared to $2 billion CAD in Natural Gas. Oil and NGLs are clearly the major financial drivers of CNQ.

Clearly, the market initially understood the benefit of higher oil prices to CNQ, and investors drove its price upward strongly along with many other major oil and natural gas producers.

Why is CNQ positioned better than most of them to reward shareholders from this temporary oil price situation? It all comes down to how CNQ is focused on rewarding shareholders. CNQ has a structure that, when its net debt falls below a certain amount, more and more of its cash flow is directed towards shareholders. In their last earnings release, they adjusted that ratio to be more favorable towards common shareholders.

According to management, their net debt sat at approximately $16 billion at the end of 2025. They were able to raise their dividend to common shareholders in March of this year to further reward shareholders. Free cash flow is returned to shareholders in the form of share buybacks and debt reduction on top of the regularly growing common dividend that CNQ has been providing shareholders.

This structure allows them to reward shareholders rapidly when excess cash flow is produced. To understand the extent to which this would be beneficial to shareholders, we must look at the sensitivity of their free cash flow. Thankfully, CNQ provides a clear outline of how sensitive free cash flow would be versus various oil pricing situations.

If WTI is priced near $85 a barrel for an extended period of time, its free cash flow is 91% higher than if it were at $65. Currently, as we've seen above, WTI is priced in the $90-a-barrel range, meaning there is nearly a 100% benefit to their free cash flow. This excess cash flow would be distributed to shareholders on a prorated basis. Considering that net debt sat near $16 billion, we are right on the threshold of 60% and 75% levels of this additional cash flow going directly to shareholders.

Furthermore, operational cash flow expectations for CNQ have been rising as the year goes on and oil prices remain elevated. This cash flow is the lifeblood of the company, and as that expectation rises, so does the fair value expectation we have for CNQ over the long term.

The question will be how CNQ chooses to predominantly reward shareholders with this excess cash flow?

  • If they continue to cut down on their net debt, we would see rewards spread over the long run.
  • If they decide to do buybacks, they are limited to how much they are allowed to buy back as a Canadian company. Currently, CNQ is authorized to buy back up to 10% of its public float from March 2026 to March 2027.
