This would require $8.3 billion at current prices, equivalent to all of their shareholder returns in 2025 (inclusive of common dividends, share buybacks, and excess debt retirement). Ironically, if WTI prices remain elevated, they'd potentially have the excess cash flow to nearly do so.
  • If they decide to do a special dividend, we'd see a one-time bonus cash payout and likely an extremely elevated share price for a limited time.

In all scenarios, common shareholders see strong benefits immediately. Net-debt reduction would allow the benefits to be doled out over a longer, more level period of time, likely boosting the expected total return of CNQ over the long run. A rapid buyback of shares would cause a strong price increase in their trading values and a reevaluation of their fair value from all per-share values growing rapidly as shares are retired. A special dividend is always a welcome one-time reward from an unusual oil pricing situation.

In the end, my personal preference would be to see an increase in buybacks and debt retirement versus a one-time special dividend. Retirement of debt and shares produces a more sustainable long-term ability for them to grow their dividend and to reward shareholders, versus a one-time large payout. In the event of a one-time large payout, we would likely see a large price swing as investors buy shares simply to try to collect the payout and then sell out afterward. Either way, earning forecasts for CNQ are likely muted compared to the reality of the benefit they will receive from higher oil prices overall because of the Iran War.

The CNQ releases its next earnings during the pre-market hours of May 5. Oil prices started to climb heavily after the close of the first quarter this year, meaning that this quarter's earnings are not likely to be extremely spectacular. I expect a focus on the events that have occurred in the time frame thereafter and the rewards that CNQ has planned for its shareholders. This is something most investors are not considering: the fact that CNQ is primed to reward them heavily from their increased cash flow from higher oil prices. The longer oil prices remain elevated, the greater the basket available for rewarding shareholders becomes. Once oil prices return to prior levels, CNQ continues to have strong operations and a low breakeven price. This allows them to continue to be a spectacular long-term investment.

Conclusion

CNQ falls into a rare group of oil and gas producers that have focused on shareholder returns more than on growing production. If we consider their low break-even point per barrel of oil for their operations, we can understand that as a very insulated oil and gas company, meaning that there isn't great regional risk to their production because they are based in Canada, we're able to benefit from this increased volatility of oil prices for as long as it lasts. The longer that oil prices remain elevated because of the conflict in Iran, the greater the amount of rewards in free cash flow that CNQ generates, and when they have more free cash flow, shareholders have more rewards in the long run.

Since our initial coverage of them back in December, CNQ has returned over 40% as total return compared to the market's muted 4.6% returns. We expect that CNQ may not have that much gas left in the tank for the share price unless there is phenomenal growth developed by the excess free cash flow rewards. This is why I think it's a great time to "Buy" now, before earnings, because when everyone hears of the rewards that they have available to shareholders, they'll start crowding in.

It's this kind of clarity above the chaos of day-to-day life that we provide to Dividend King members via data-driven, facts-first fundamental analysis.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Everus Construction Group: Skilled Labor Shortage Will Drive This Market Leader Higher
data.description:
Everus Construction Group (ECG) is a specialty construction contractor, recently separated from a general contractor by the name of MDU Construction Services Group.

Everus operates within two critical segments:

  • Electrical & Mechanical: Includes construction and maintenance of electrical and communication wiring, as well as mechanical piping and services.
  • Transmission & Distribution: Includes services for private and public companies focusing on generation and distribution of electricity, which covers overhead and underground electric power.

Since the date of the spinoff, Everus has seen a tremendous expansion in its E&M segment, which now represents a staggering 88% of backlog due to the expansion related to the data center infrastructure buildout.

I strongly believe that Everus Construction Group is a market leader for multiple reasons, as this article will highlight. These reasons include:

  1. A shortage in skilled electrical labor
  2. Acquisition tailwinds
  3. Relative valuation

Based on the above reasons, I would like to issue a "Strong Buy" rating on ECG, even after some potential risks around execution.

General Outlook on the AI Supercycle

Starting off, I would like to mention that it is important to recognize that the AI industry is cyclical. This extends to every part of the industry that benefits from increased capital expenditures, which extends to not only the Nvidias and Taiwan Semiconductors of the world, but also to more specialized companies such as Everus. However, it is also important to understand the size and timing of this cycle.

Coatue Capital Management depicts the following visualization:

It is an investor's job to understand where we are at in the cycle of AI.

Currently, we are seeing that demand is not only accelerating but also increasing in duration. For example, companies like Micron (MU), a key supplier of memory chips (a core component of any data center), have signed their first five-year Strategic Customer Agreement. Agreements like these speak volumes to the demand and commitment from the industry to continue the expansion of the AI buildout, specifically within memory, which has typically relied on short term SCAs, such as 1 year or shorter.

Going away from memory and going closer to an industry more related to the business of Everus, I would like to discuss the shortage within electrical equipment.

As reported by the Financial Post, the US is facing a major shortage of electrical equipment, specifically transformers and switchgears. This is forcing companies to rely on foreign-made equipment, often of Chinese make.

This is far from ideal, as importing equipment usually adds to timelines, can create supply chain risks, and is often more expensive due to shipping costs. However, this is the situation that these data center operators are currently finding themselves in. They are forced to take on these risks just to get ahead of their neighbor, who is also adding megawatts of data center capacity next door.

The above two shortages are not the first and not the last. To me, it is very apparent that we are nowhere near the top of this cycle and that the inflection point of a deceleration in demand is not on the horizon.

With this information, an investor can, in fact, say that this cycle is indeed different.

By establishing the above, I can now open myself to discuss and invest in a name like Everus, which to me relies on the very above shortages. This is not only due to the fact that they directly support the case for continued acceleration in data center buildout, but also because continued acceleration is bound to continue or even exacerbate investor sentiment within this segment.

Skilled Labor Is Becoming a Key Bottleneck In AI Buildout

According to many sources, which I will discuss below, shortages within the AI expansion are branching out from only the sectors that we tend to hear about daily. As demand picks up more and more, more and more sectors appear with significant pricing power due to shortages, including sectors such as memory, advanced packaging, and electrical equipment. In my job as an analyst, I aim to find the next bottleneck. To me, one of these bottlenecks that we may encounter is around skilled labor. Specifically, I will be discussing electrician labor shortages.

Looking at the construction of a typical data center, sources indicate that skilled electrical labor accounts for roughly 45% to 70% of total data center construction costs. There is no debate to consider this a key step within the entire life cycle of a data center.

To be able to keep up the current momentum of data center construction in the US, Associated Builders and Contractors, a trade organization, estimates that the US economy will require an additional net 349,000 new workers in 2026, followed by an additional 456,000 new workers. The main driver in this strong growth is the resurgence in construction spending, driven by the concentrated focus of data center expansion.

The same report goes on to say the following:

Demand for electricians capable of precision wiring has surged due to the rapid increase in data center construction. Recent industry efforts to accelerate skilled worker development have helped, but the industry is effectively swimming upstream. Approximately one-fifth of all electricians are over 55. Worker shortages also remain more severe in areas associated with industrial megaprojects, including semiconductor fabrication facilities.

On top of the above information, Brookings indicates that one large data center project can absorb as many as 1,500 skilled trade workers, meaning that every new data center announcement puts more pressure on an industry that is already constrained with crackdowns on immigration by the Trump administration. After all, as the same report indicates, roughly 30% of skilled trade workers in the US are immigrants, with some being undocumented.

As we all know, AI data centers will put tremendous strain on the grid infrastructure of the US. However, it is important to consider the scale and growth rate of this trend. Looking at the information we have presented today, the growth is truly unprecedented.

McKinsey indicates that electricity demand for data centers will grow to 606 TWh by 2030, from a current base of just under 300 TWh. This is expected to represent nearly 12% of US power demand.

Why Everus Construction Group?

Thus, taking the above information into account, I believe that there is significant merit to considering a data center and grid expansion-focused construction services company as a potential addition to the portfolio.

When considering a company within such a sector, investors need to clearly understand what they are investing in. Although I believe that Everus is a significant beneficiary of the above trends, as will be described below, it is also important to understand that by design, the moat of a company such as Everus is actually quite small. After all, Everus and their competitors do not have a significant technology moat. This is the reason for the low margins in the sector.

That said, that's not necessarily a bad thing. After all, the shortages mentioned above actually significantly strengthen the moat of the company (by preventing new companies from entering). In effect, this serves as a tailwind for multiple expansions as investors price in a business that possesses a resource that is difficult to access, resulting in a barrier to entry.

Thus, to be able to participate in this trend, I look for the following:

  1. Pure play on the trend I am looking to capture
  2. Strong execution and visibility into future revenue
  3. Experience within the sector

Everus possesses all 3 traits, in my opinion.

It is a pure-play in the sense that out of all competitors, it is the most exposed to data centers, high-tech (semiconductor fabrication, etc.), and grid expansion. While most companies do not break out backlog per end market, Everus stated the following on their last earnings call:

Our record backlog really provides us a clear line of sight for 2026, and some of those projects go into 2027. And if you look at where those backlog contributions are coming from sequentially, it's not just data centers. It is largely data centers, but it's also hospitality and high-tech, it's substation and transmission as well. - Jeff Thiede, Everus CEO

Additionally, the below can be seen from the company's latest earnings presentation, where we can see that data centers represent up to 55% of TTM revenues.

When looking at strong execution, this is evident in many aspects with Everus. In 2025, the company raised full-year guidance two times - in Q2 and Q3 2025. Backlog has expanded significantly, with approximately 16% growth, despite record execution in 2025.

Additionally, margins have expanded across the board, with both net income and EBITDA margins for the year increasing by 40 bps. While this may seem trivial, due to the low margin nature of the sector, this represents a significant uplift to earnings.

Lastly, I look for a company that has a strong track record to their name. It is important to mention that although Everus was only trading on the stock market for just over a year and a half, it was spun off from a company by the name of MDU Construction Services Group, which has publicly traded since 1996.

Competition Is Too Diversified

Of course, other competition exists and must be mentioned. The largest player in the sector is Quanta Services (PWR), a construction services provider with a market cap roughly 15 times larger, at ~$95B, as of April 24th, 2026.

Although it seems that PWR has been executing extremely well, this company is too diversified for me to confidently say that it captures my thesis well. From the company's 2026 investors day presentation:

As can be seen above, Quanta is one of the main players within the expansion of the US grid system, with the Electric Grid & Gas Utility segment making up around half of 2025 revenue. While this does play into my thesis, the Technology & Load Center segment, which includes data centers, is only 10% of revenue.

While it is no doubt a high-quality company, it does not suit the thesis as well as Everus.

Another player to include is MYR Group (MYRG), which is also highly focused on T&D (Transmission & Distribution), while also being significantly more diversified between not only data centers but also healthcare, airport projects, transportation, clean energy, and EV charging.

Thus, it seems that Everus is as close to a pure play on data center construction as one can find.

Discounted Valuation

Comparing typical metrics, such as GAAP Price/Earnings over time, shows that Everus is by far the cheapest, especially when compared to a giant, such as PWR.

Despite this, analyst estimates, as presented by Seeking Alpha, indicate that Everus is the market leader in revenue growth and close to par with other players.

Ticker Revenue Growth (FWD) Diluted Net Income Growth (FWD)
ECG 17.64% 17.90%
PWR 16.70% 19.70%
MYRG 9.6% 23.8%

It is also important to consider that Everus has handily beat analyst estimates in previous quarters, with EPS on average 39% above estimates in the last 4 quarters, as per Seeking Alpha.

With the above considered, this also doesn't mean that Everus will necessarily re-rate to similar valuation levels as peers. Peers deserve a premium over Everus due to diversification in both end market and geography, as well as scale (all of which adds to dependability of cash flows).

However, this does mean that Everus is still a strong opportunity to participate in a pure-play data center construction company, with a strong track record of execution. As Everus continues to execute, it can be expected that valuation will re-rate to some extent with the growth of the company.

Acquisition of SE&M

In line with the above thesis of valuation expansion, Everus has acquired a sizeable construction company operating in the Southeast United States.

The construction industry has historically been very dependent on M&A in order to expand, and this transaction is no different.

That said, it is important to note that this acquisition will allow Everus to significantly expand into the Southeast United States, an area that is currently underrepresented by Everus, and one that management has stated they want to expand into if the right opportunity presents itself.

If you look at where our footprint is, there's opportunities across the country, particularly as you kind of get down to the South and Southeast. I'm not saying that that's where we're headed, but those are opportunities if we find the right work and the right set of jobs to expand on there. - Maximillian Marcy, Everus CFO

The acquisition cost the company $158M, which is expected to be funded by cash on hand.

In terms of revenue, SE&M delivered $109M of revenue in 2025, with EBITDA margins "in the high teens". Running a quick back-of-the-envelope calculation and assuming 17.5% EBITDA margins, this would put SE&M at around $19.1 TTM EBITDA, meaning that the acquisition was around 8.3x EV/EBITDA.

In the previous earnings call, management mentioned that typical multiples for M&A in recent times are around 9-10x, meaning that this deal came at a discount, while also expanding Everus's presence in a historically underdeveloped region.

Investors should rejoice, as it seems that Everus found the deal they were looking for.

Risk

No thesis is risk free.

The main risks to me emerge around execution. Specifically, I would like to mention margins.

In an industry such as construction, margins are incredibly tight. This means that even a small deterioration can have a tremendous impact on valuation and, as such, share price.

I recommend investors monitor net income and EBITDA margins, especially as Everus aims to integrate a sizeable, $158M company into its operations. Challenges within this space may push me to think about competition as alternative investments.

Conclusion

Taken together, Everus Construction Group presents an excellent investment opportunity.

As the AI industry seems to be expanding at full throttle, news of new bottlenecks within the space appears to be popping up seemingly every other week, and Everus is well positioned to capture the next bottleneck, being skilled electrical labor.

As compared to the competition, Everus presents itself as a pure play on data center construction, and offers an excellent track record of execution, with a strong backlog and solid inorganic growth opportunities on the horizon.

Thus, I am very bullish on the construction services sector, where I see specifically Everus Construction Group come out as a market leader in 2026.

I would like to initiate my rating of ECG with a "Strong Buy" rating.
